exam.description: Original, SAT-style practice for the Digital SAT, covering the Reading and Writing section and the Math section. All content is original and contains no copyrighted College Board material.

teaching_card.title: Finding the Main Idea
teaching_card.body: The main idea is the single point a passage is built to make. Look for what every sentence supports, not a small detail. A good summary captures the whole passage without adding outside claims.
key_points: Ask: what is the passage mostly about?
Avoid choices that are true but too narrow.
Reject choices that go beyond the passage.

teaching_card.title: Making Inferences
teaching_card.body: An inference is a conclusion the passage supports but does not state outright. Stay close to the text: the best answer is the one the evidence forces, not the one that merely sounds reasonable.
key_points: Base inferences on stated evidence.
Avoid going further than the text allows.
Eliminate choices the passage contradicts.

teaching_card.title: Command of Evidence
teaching_card.body: These questions ask which detail best supports a claim. Find the specific sentence or data point that directly backs the claim — not one that is merely related to the topic.
key_points: Match the evidence to the exact claim.
Specific, on-point details beat general ones.
Beware details that are true but off-topic.

teaching_card.title: Text Structure and Purpose
teaching_card.body: Every passage is built a certain way — by contrast, cause and effect, problem and solution, and so on — to serve a purpose. Ask why the author included a part and how the whole is organized.
key_points: Identify the organizing pattern.
Ask what a sentence accomplishes in context.
Match purpose to the passage's overall aim.

teaching_card.title: Words in Context
teaching_card.body: A word's meaning on the test depends on its sentence. Predict a substitute that fits the context, then pick the choice closest to your prediction. Common words often carry less common meanings here.
key_points: Read the whole sentence, not just the word.
Predict a meaning before reading choices.
Watch for secondary meanings of familiar words.

teaching_card.title: Cross-Text Connections
teaching_card.body: Paired passages present two views on one topic. Pin down each author's claim, then judge how they relate: agreement, disagreement, or one qualifying the other. Predict how one author would answer the other.
key_points: Summarize each text's main claim first.
Determine the relationship between the claims.
Anticipate how one author would respond to the other.

teaching_card.title: Sentence Boundaries
teaching_card.body: An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (a comma splice) or by nothing (a run-on). Use a period, a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.
key_points: A comma alone can't join two independent clauses.
Use a period or semicolon between independent clauses.
A comma + FANBOYS conjunction also works.

teaching_card.title: Sentence Boundaries (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (a comma splice) or by nothing (a run-on). Use a period, a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.
key_points: A comma alone can't join two independent clauses.
Use a period or semicolon between independent clauses.
A comma + FANBOYS conjunction also works.

teaching_card.title: Sentence Boundaries (Level 3)
teaching_card.body: An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (a comma splice) or by nothing (a run-on). Use a period, a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.
key_points: A comma alone can't join two independent clauses.
Use a period or semicolon between independent clauses.
A comma + FANBOYS conjunction also works.

teaching_card.title: Sentence Boundaries (Level 4)
teaching_card.body: An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone (a comma splice) or by nothing (a run-on). Use a period, a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.
key_points: A comma alone can't join two independent clauses.
Use a period or semicolon between independent clauses.
A comma + FANBOYS conjunction also works.

teaching_card.title: Subject-Verb Agreement
teaching_card.body: A verb must match its subject in number. Ignore words between the subject and verb — especially prepositional phrases — which often hide the true subject and bait you into the wrong number.
key_points: Find the true subject, ignoring in-between phrases.
Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Watch indefinite pronouns like 'each' (singular).

teaching_card.title: Subject-Verb Agreement (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: A verb must match its subject in number. Ignore words between the subject and verb — especially prepositional phrases — which often hide the true subject and bait you into the wrong number.
key_points: Find the true subject, ignoring in-between phrases.
Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Watch indefinite pronouns like 'each' (singular).

teaching_card.body: A verb must match its subject in number. Ignore words between the subject and verb — especially prepositional phrases — which often hide the true subject and bait you into the wrong number.
key_points: Find the true subject, ignoring in-between phrases.
Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Watch indefinite pronouns like 'each' (singular).

teaching_card.body: A verb must match its subject in number. Ignore words between the subject and verb — especially prepositional phrases — which often hide the true subject and bait you into the wrong number.
key_points: Find the true subject, ignoring in-between phrases.
Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Watch indefinite pronouns like 'each' (singular).

teaching_card.title: Punctuation and Possessives
teaching_card.body: Possession is shown with an apostrophe: add 's to a singular noun, and just an apostrophe after a plural noun ending in -s. A plain -s makes a plural, not a possessive.
key_points: Singular possessive: add 's.
Plural ending in -s: add only an apostrophe.
No apostrophe makes a noun plural, not possessive.

teaching_card.title: Punctuation and Possessives (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Possession is shown with an apostrophe: add 's to a singular noun, and just an apostrophe after a plural noun ending in -s. A plain -s makes a plural, not a possessive.
key_points: Singular possessive: add 's.
Plural ending in -s: add only an apostrophe.
No apostrophe makes a noun plural, not possessive.

teaching_card.title: Punctuation and Possessives (Level 3)
teaching_card.body: Possession is shown with an apostrophe: add 's to a singular noun, and just an apostrophe after a plural noun ending in -s. A plain -s makes a plural, not a possessive.
key_points: Singular possessive: add 's.
Plural ending in -s: add only an apostrophe.
No apostrophe makes a noun plural, not possessive.

teaching_card.title: Pronoun Case
teaching_card.body: Choose pronoun case by the pronoun's job. Subjects take I, he, she, they, who; objects take me, him, her, them, whom. In compounds, test the pronoun alone to hear the right case.
key_points: Subjective case for subjects (I, who).
Objective case for objects (me, whom).
In compounds, drop the other name to check.

teaching_card.title: Pronoun Case (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Choose pronoun case by the pronoun's job. Subjects take I, he, she, they, who; objects take me, him, her, them, whom. In compounds, test the pronoun alone to hear the right case.
key_points: Subjective case for subjects (I, who).
Objective case for objects (me, whom).
In compounds, drop the other name to check.

teaching_card.title: Modifier Placement
teaching_card.body: An introductory phrase must modify the noun that immediately follows it. If it doesn't, the modifier 'dangles' and the sentence says something illogical. Place the right noun right after the phrase.
key_points: Put the modified noun right after the phrase.
Check that the opening phrase logically fits that noun.
Dangling modifiers create unintended meanings.

teaching_card.title: Transitions
teaching_card.body: Transitions signal the logical link between ideas: contrast (however), cause and effect (therefore), addition (moreover), example (for instance). Decide the relationship first, then choose the matching transition.
key_points: Identify the relationship between the two ideas.
However/yet = contrast; therefore = result.
Moreover = addition; for example = illustration.

teaching_card.title: Transitions (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Transitions signal the logical link between ideas: contrast (however), cause and effect (therefore), addition (moreover), example (for instance). Decide the relationship first, then choose the matching transition.
key_points: Identify the relationship between the two ideas.
However/yet = contrast; therefore = result.
Moreover = addition; for example = illustration.

teaching_card.title: Concision
teaching_card.body: Good writing says it once. Eliminate redundancy (repeating the same idea in different words) and wordy filler. The best choice is usually the shortest one that keeps the full meaning and stays grammatical.
key_points: Cut words that repeat an idea.
Prefer the shortest clear, grammatical option.
Don't delete meaning — only redundancy.

teaching_card.title: Concision (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Good writing says it once. Eliminate redundancy (repeating the same idea in different words) and wordy filler. The best choice is usually the shortest one that keeps the full meaning and stays grammatical.
key_points: Cut words that repeat an idea.
Prefer the shortest clear, grammatical option.
Don't delete meaning — only redundancy.

teaching_card.title: Rhetorical Synthesis
teaching_card.body: You're given notes and a specific goal (introduce, emphasize, compare). Pick the sentence that uses the relevant notes to accomplish that exact goal — not just any accurate sentence.
key_points: Read the goal carefully before the choices.
Choose the option that serves that goal.
Accurate but off-goal choices are traps.

teaching_card.title: Solving Linear Equations
teaching_card.body: A linear equation keeps the variable to the first power. To solve, undo what was done to the variable using inverse operations, keeping both sides balanced: add/subtract to move constants, then multiply/divide to isolate the variable.
key_points: Do the same operation to both sides.
Move constants first, then divide by the coefficient.
Check by substituting your answer back in.

teaching_card.title: Solving Linear Equations (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: A linear equation keeps the variable to the first power. To solve, undo what was done to the variable using inverse operations, keeping both sides balanced: add/subtract to move constants, then multiply/divide to isolate the variable.
key_points: Do the same operation to both sides.
Move constants first, then divide by the coefficient.
Check by substituting your answer back in.

teaching_card.title: Linear Inequalities
teaching_card.body: Solve an inequality like an equation, but remember one rule: if you multiply or divide both sides by a negative number, flip the inequality sign. The solution is a range of values, not a single one.
key_points: Isolate the variable as with equations.
Flip the sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative.
≤ and ≥ include the endpoint; < and > do not.

teaching_card.title: Systems of Equations
teaching_card.body: A system gives two equations with two unknowns. Use substitution (solve one equation for a variable and plug in) or elimination (add or subtract the equations to cancel a variable). The solution makes both equations true at once.
key_points: Substitution: isolate one variable, then substitute.
Elimination: combine equations to cancel a variable.
A solution satisfies both equations simultaneously.
teaching_card.worked_example: x+y=7 and x−y=1 → add: 2x=8 → x=4, y=3.

teaching_card.title: Systems of Equations (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: A system gives two equations with two unknowns. Use substitution (solve one equation for a variable and plug in) or elimination (add or subtract the equations to cancel a variable). The solution makes both equations true at once.
key_points: Substitution: isolate one variable, then substitute.
Elimination: combine equations to cancel a variable.
A solution satisfies both equations simultaneously.
teaching_card.worked_example: x+y=7 and x−y=1 → add: 2x=8 → x=4, y=3.

teaching_card.title: Function Notation
teaching_card.body: f(x) is a rule that turns an input x into an output. To evaluate f at a number, substitute that number everywhere x appears and simplify. Mind the order of operations, especially with squares and negatives.
key_points: f(k) means substitute k for x.
Square before multiplying; handle negatives carefully.
The output depends only on the input you plug in.

teaching_card.title: Function Notation (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: f(x) is a rule that turns an input x into an output. To evaluate f at a number, substitute that number everywhere x appears and simplify. Mind the order of operations, especially with squares and negatives.
key_points: f(k) means substitute k for x.
Square before multiplying; handle negatives carefully.
The output depends only on the input you plug in.

teaching_card.title: Quadratic Equations
teaching_card.body: A quadratic has an x² term. Many factor into (x−r₁)(x−r₂)=0, whose solutions (roots) are r₁ and r₂. The sum of the roots equals −b/a and the product equals c/a, a useful shortcut on the test.
key_points: Set each factor equal to zero to find the roots.
Sum of roots = −b/a; product of roots = c/a.
Check signs carefully when factoring.

teaching_card.title: Quadratic Equations (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: A quadratic has an x² term. Many factor into (x−r₁)(x−r₂)=0, whose solutions (roots) are r₁ and r₂. The sum of the roots equals −b/a and the product equals c/a, a useful shortcut on the test.
key_points: Set each factor equal to zero to find the roots.
Sum of roots = −b/a; product of roots = c/a.
Check signs carefully when factoring.

teaching_card.title: Exponents and Radicals
teaching_card.body: Exponent rules let you combine powers. Multiplying powers with the same base adds exponents; dividing subtracts them; a power of a power multiplies them. A radical is a fractional exponent.
key_points: aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ (add when multiplying).
aᵐ/aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ (subtract when dividing).
(aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ (multiply for a power of a power).

teaching_card.title: Exponents and Radicals (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Exponent rules let you combine powers. Multiplying powers with the same base adds exponents; dividing subtracts them; a power of a power multiplies them. A radical is a fractional exponent.
key_points: aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ (add when multiplying).
aᵐ/aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ (subtract when dividing).
(aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ (multiply for a power of a power).

teaching_card.title: Polynomial Expressions
teaching_card.body: To multiply two binomials, use FOIL: First, Outer, Inner, Last, then combine like terms. The coefficient of x comes from the Outer and Inner products added together.
key_points: FOIL: First, Outer, Inner, Last.
Combine like terms after multiplying.
The x² term comes from First; the constant from Last.

teaching_card.title: Percentages and Ratios
teaching_card.body: A percent is a part out of 100. To find a percent of a number, multiply by the percent over 100. Percent change is the difference divided by the original amount, times 100.
key_points: Percent change = (new − old)/old × 100.
Always divide by the original value for percent change.

teaching_card.title: Percentages and Ratios (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: A percent is a part out of 100. To find a percent of a number, multiply by the percent over 100. Percent change is the difference divided by the original amount, times 100.
key_points: Percent change = (new − old)/old × 100.
Always divide by the original value for percent change.

teaching_card.title: Proportions and Rates
teaching_card.body: A proportion sets two ratios equal. Cross-multiply to solve for an unknown. Rates compare two quantities with different units, like miles per hour; scale them up or down proportionally.
key_points: Set equal ratios: a/b = c/d.
Cross-multiply to solve: a·d = b·c.
Keep units consistent on each side.

teaching_card.title: Reading Data from Tables
teaching_card.body: Tables and graphs pack information into rows and columns. Read the headers first so you know what each value means, then locate exactly the value(s) the question asks for before doing any arithmetic.
key_points: Read titles and headers before the numbers.
Match the question to the right row/column.
Totals, maxima, and differences are common asks.
teaching_card.worked_example: To find a total, add the relevant column's entries.

teaching_card.title: Reading Data from Tables (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Tables and graphs pack information into rows and columns. Read the headers first so you know what each value means, then locate exactly the value(s) the question asks for before doing any arithmetic.
key_points: Read titles and headers before the numbers.
Match the question to the right row/column.
Totals, maxima, and differences are common asks.
teaching_card.worked_example: To find a total, add the relevant column's entries.

teaching_card.title: Mean, Median, and Range
teaching_card.body: The mean is the sum divided by the count. The median is the middle value when data are in order. The range is the largest value minus the smallest. Each summarizes a data set differently.
key_points: Mean = sum ÷ count.
Median = middle value (order the data first).
Range = max − min.

teaching_card.title: Mean, Median, and Range (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: The mean is the sum divided by the count. The median is the middle value when data are in order. The range is the largest value minus the smallest. Each summarizes a data set differently.
key_points: Mean = sum ÷ count.
Median = middle value (order the data first).
Range = max − min.

teaching_card.title: Basic Probability
teaching_card.body: Probability measures how likely an event is, from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). For equally likely outcomes, it is the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes.
key_points: P(event) = favorable ÷ total.
Use the total count in the denominator.
P(not event) = 1 − P(event).

teaching_card.title: Area and Volume
teaching_card.body: Area measures a flat region; volume measures space inside a solid. Rectangle area = base × height; triangle area = ½ × base × height; box volume = length × width × height.
key_points: Rectangle area = b·h.
Triangle area = ½·b·h.
Box volume = l·w·h.

teaching_card.title: Area and Volume (Level 2)
teaching_card.body: Area measures a flat region; volume measures space inside a solid. Rectangle area = base × height; triangle area = ½ × base × height; box volume = length × width × height.
key_points: Rectangle area = b·h.
Triangle area = ½·b·h.
Box volume = l·w·h.

teaching_card.title: Circles
teaching_card.body: For a circle of radius r: the circumference (distance around) is 2πr, and the area is πr². The diameter is twice the radius. Answers are often left 'in terms of π.'
key_points: Circumference = 2πr.
Area = πr².
Diameter = 2r.

teaching_card.title: Right Triangles
teaching_card.body: In a right triangle, the Pythagorean theorem links the legs a and b to the hypotenuse c: a² + b² = c². Recognizing common triples like 3-4-5 saves time.
key_points: a² + b² = c² (c is the hypotenuse).
The hypotenuse is the longest side.

teaching_card.title: Right-Triangle Trigonometry
teaching_card.body: SOH-CAH-TOA relates an acute angle to side ratios: sine = opposite/hypotenuse, cosine = adjacent/hypotenuse, tangent = opposite/adjacent. Identify the sides relative to the angle first.
key_points: sin = opposite/hypotenuse.
cos = adjacent/hypotenuse.
tan = opposite/adjacent.
teaching_card.worked_example: In a 3-4-5 triangle, sin θ = 3/5 if 3 is opposite θ.

questions.prompt: If 5x + 14 = 39, what is the value of x?

choices.rationale: This adds 1 too many; 5·6+14 ≠ 39.
This is one less than the solution; recheck the subtraction.
This is 5x, not x; you still must divide by 5.

questions.explanation: Subtract 14 from both sides: 5x = 25. Divide both sides by 5: x = 5.

questions.prompt: If 2x + 8 = 20, what is the value of x?

choices.rationale: This is one less than the solution; recheck the subtraction.
Correct: 2·6+8=20.
This is 2x, not x; you still must divide by 2.
This adds 1 too many; 2·7+8 ≠ 20.

questions.explanation: Subtract 8 from both sides: 2x = 12. Divide both sides by 2: x = 6.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: A frozen seed vault preserves crop diversity as a backup against future losses.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains the vault as a rarely used backup that protects crop variety.

choices.text: The seed vault is opened and used every day.
choices.rationale: The keepers hope it is rarely needed.

choices.text: Frozen seeds lose the ability to sprout within a year.
choices.rationale: The cold lets seeds sprout even after decades.

choices.text: The vault stores gold rather than seeds.
choices.rationale: It values seeds 'more than gold,' figuratively.

questions.explanation: The passage frames the vault as a safeguard for crop diversity.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Automation made lighthouses more reliable but ended the human role of the keeper.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage contrasts improved reliability with the loss of the human keeper.
The lights 'still shine,' more reliably than before.
Keepers performed essential tasks for centuries.

choices.text: Modern lighthouses still require nightly keepers.
choices.rationale: Automation withdrew the keepers.

questions.explanation: The passage balances technical gain against a human loss.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Glass is endlessly recyclable in principle, but flawed systems keep much of it from being reused.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage contrasts glass's ideal recyclability with system failures.

choices.text: Glass loses quality each time it is recycled.
choices.rationale: The passage says glass keeps its quality when remade.

choices.text: Glass can never be recycled at all.
choices.rationale: It says glass can be remade again and again.
Color sorting is offered as a solution, not an obstacle to recycling.

questions.explanation: The passage's point is the gap between glass's ideal recyclability and imperfect systems.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: The honeybee's waggle dance is a symbolic code that communicates a food source's direction and distance.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how the dance encodes direction and distance.

choices.text: Honeybees can speak to one another in sounds.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses communication 'without sound.'

choices.text: Honeybees find flowers only by luck.
choices.rationale: The dance communicates location, not luck.

choices.text: The waggle dance has no consistent meaning.
choices.rationale: The dance reliably encodes specific information.

questions.explanation: The passage frames the waggle dance as a precise symbolic language.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Accurate maps were achieved gradually, replacing guesses with hard-won measurements.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage traces the replacement of guesses by measurements and honors the effort behind it.

choices.text: Old mapmakers genuinely believed in sea monsters.
choices.rationale: The passage says monsters were decoration 'less from belief.'

choices.text: Maps have never contained any errors.
choices.rationale: The passage describes early errors.
Explorers corrected maps; they did not prefer errors.

questions.explanation: The passage's point is the slow, effortful move from guesswork to accuracy.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Fog nets can supply useful water in suitable coastal areas, within real limits.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage presents fog harvesting as useful where conditions fit, while noting limits.

choices.text: Fog nets can replace all other water sources everywhere.
choices.rationale: The passage calls it a supplement, not a universal replacement.

choices.text: Fog harvesting requires complex machinery and electricity.
choices.rationale: It relies on gravity and simple mesh, not complex machinery.

choices.text: Fog never contains enough moisture to collect.
choices.rationale: The passage says nets can collect many liters.

questions.explanation: The balanced claim is that fog nets work well in the right setting but are not a cure-all.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: The Library of Alexandria pursued a sweeping goal of universal knowledge whose ideal outlived it.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage stresses the ambition of universal collection and the lasting power of that idea.

choices.text: The library was destroyed in a single, well-documented fire.
choices.rationale: The passage says the single-fire story oversimplifies a slow decline.

choices.text: Only local scholars were allowed to use the library.
choices.rationale: Scholars came from distant lands.

choices.text: The library refused to copy any scrolls.
choices.rationale: The library actively copied scrolls.

questions.explanation: The passage's emphasis is the ambitious ideal and its endurance, not a single dramatic end.

stimulus.title: A House for Every Book
stimulus.text: The ancient library at Alexandria aimed at something audacious: to gather a copy of every book in the world. Ships docking at the harbor were searched, and any scrolls aboard were copied; sometimes the library kept the original and returned the copy. Scholars from distant lands came to read, argue, and write, and the collection may have held hundreds of thousands of scrolls. We know less than we would like about how the library declined, and popular stories of a single great fire oversimplify a slow loss across centuries. Still, the idea behind it endured. The dream of one place that holds all knowledge has reappeared, in new forms, ever since.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Plastic breaks into tiny, widespread particles that are hard to remove, making prevention the best response.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage describes the spread and persistence of microplastics and favors prevention.

choices.text: Microplastics are easy to filter from the ocean.
choices.rationale: The passage says filtering is nearly impossible.

choices.text: Scientists have proven microplastics are harmless.
choices.rationale: It says health effects are not yet clear.

choices.text: Plastic disappears completely when exposed to sunlight.
choices.rationale: It says plastic fragments rather than vanishes.

questions.explanation: The passage links persistence and spread to a prevention-first conclusion.

stimulus.title: Small Pieces, Wide Reach
stimulus.text: Plastic does not simply vanish. Exposed to sunlight and waves, a bottle or bag breaks into ever-smaller fragments until the pieces are too tiny to see. These microplastics have turned up in river water, in Arctic ice, and even in the bodies of fish and birds. Because the particles are so small, filtering them out of the ocean is nearly impossible. Researchers caution that the full effects on health are not yet clear, and they warn against easy conclusions. What is clear is that the cheapest fix is also the simplest: keeping plastic out of the environment in the first place, before it can shatter into pieces no net can catch.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: The bicycle evolved through gradual improvements into a popular means of independent travel.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage traces stepwise improvements leading to the bicycle's popularity and freedom of movement.

choices.text: The first bicycle was an immediate success.
choices.rationale: The first version was mocked and faded, not an immediate success.
The passage says it needed no horse.

choices.text: Pedals were part of the very first bicycle design.
choices.rationale: The earliest version had no pedals.

questions.explanation: The passage's arc is incremental improvement culminating in widespread, independent travel.

stimulus.title: The Machine That Walked
stimulus.text: The earliest ancestor of the bicycle had no pedals at all. Built in 1817, it was a wooden frame on two wheels that a rider straddled and pushed along with the feet, like walking while seated. People mocked it, and rutted roads made it impractical, so it soon faded. Decades passed before inventors added pedals, then a chain, then air-filled tires that smoothed the ride. Each change drew new riders, and by the 1890s the bicycle had become a craze. It offered something rare for the time: cheap, independent travel that needed no horse and no rails. For many people, especially those long denied easy movement, the machine quietly widened the boundaries of daily life.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Volcanic ash creates fertile soil, drawing farmers despite the danger of eruptions.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains the fertility-versus-danger trade-off that keeps farmers near volcanoes.

choices.text: Volcanoes are harmless to nearby villages.
choices.rationale: The passage notes a real danger, so volcanoes are not harmless.
The passage says many people do farm there.

choices.text: Potassium is the only mineral plants need.
choices.rationale: It names potassium and phosphorus, not a single required mineral.

questions.explanation: The passage balances the benefit of fertile soil against eruption risk.

stimulus.title: Gifts of the Volcano
stimulus.text: It seems strange that people would farm in the shadow of a volcano, but many do, and for good reason. When a volcano erupts, it scatters ash rich in minerals such as potassium and phosphorus. Over years, this ash breaks down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. Crops planted in it can grow unusually well, which is why slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages. The bargain is uneasy: the same mountain that feeds the fields can also threaten them. Farmers weigh the steady gift of rich soil against the rare but real danger overhead, and generation after generation, many choose to stay.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Sleep is an active process that supports memory and brain maintenance, in the right amount.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage presents sleep as active and beneficial within an optimal range.

choices.text: Staying awake longer always improves memory.
choices.rationale: The experiment shows the opposite of this claim.

choices.text: The brain does nothing useful during sleep.
choices.rationale: The passage argues the brain is busy during sleep.

choices.text: More sleep is always better for performance.
choices.rationale: The passage explicitly rejects 'more is always better.'

questions.explanation: The passage's balanced claim is that sleep actively helps the brain, but only in adequate amounts.

stimulus.title: The Night Shift of the Brain
stimulus.text: Sleep can look like idleness, but the brain is busy. During deep sleep, waves of electrical activity sweep across the cortex, and researchers think these waves help move the day's memories into longer-term storage. In one line of experiments, students who slept after studying a list of words remembered more of them than students who stayed awake the same number of hours. Sleep may also clear away molecular waste that builds up while we are awake. None of this means more sleep is always better; the relationship appears to follow a curve, with both too little and too much linked to poorer performance. What the brain seems to need is enough, at the right times.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Sparrows learn local song dialects that vary by place and shift over time.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains learned, place-based dialects that change over generations.

choices.text: All white-crowned sparrows sing exactly the same song.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses variation, not sameness.

choices.text: Sparrows are born already knowing their full song.
choices.rationale: Birds must learn the song; they are not born knowing it.

choices.text: Human accents are caused by birdsong.
choices.rationale: The human-accent comparison is an analogy, not a cause.

questions.explanation: The passage's through-line is learned, drifting song dialects.

stimulus.title: Songs With Accents
stimulus.text: A white-crowned sparrow raised near San Francisco does not sing quite like one raised a hundred miles north. The basic melody is the same, but small flourishes differ from place to place, forming what biologists call song dialects. Young sparrows learn these dialects by listening to adult males during a brief window in their first months. A bird that hears no song in that window grows up to sing a thin, simplified tune. The dialects can drift over generations, much as human accents do, so a recording made decades ago may sound subtly foreign to the birds singing in the same meadow today.

questions.prompt: Which choice best captures the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: A fine instrument reveals the skill Nadia had developed through earlier struggle.
choices.rationale: Correct. The closing lines tie the fine violin to the skill she had quietly built.

choices.text: Nadia decides to quit playing the violin after the recital.
choices.rationale: She does not quit; she gains insight.

choices.text: School instruments are always better than loaners.
choices.rationale: The passage shows the loaner as finer than her battered school violin.

choices.text: Nadia's teacher refuses to let her use the loaner violin.
choices.rationale: The teacher hands her the loaner rather than refusing.

questions.explanation: The narrative resolves on the idea that her growth, not just the violin, produced the beautiful playing.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: The tardigrade survives extreme conditions by drying into a protected, revivable state.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage centers on how the tardigrade endures extremes by drying out.

choices.text: The tardigrade is the smallest animal on Earth.
choices.rationale: The passage compares its size to salt but never calls it the smallest animal.

choices.text: Scientists have completely explained the tardigrade's survival.
choices.rationale: The text says scientists are 'still untangling' the mechanism.

choices.text: The tardigrade lives only in outer space.
choices.rationale: Space is one extreme it can survive, not its home.

questions.explanation: The passage's focus is the dehydration survival strategy and its partial explanation.

stimulus.title: The Survivor
stimulus.text: Smaller than a grain of salt, the tardigrade looks almost comical, plodding on eight stubby legs. Its talent, however, is anything but ordinary. When its surroundings dry out, the creature expels most of its water and curls into a husk called a tun. In this state it can endure boiling heat, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill nearly any other animal. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it. Scientists are still untangling how the tardigrade protects its cells, but one clue is a special set of proteins that seem to harden into glass, cradling delicate structures until water returns.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Gutenberg's reusable metal type made books far easier to reproduce and spread.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage emphasizes how reusable type transformed the reproduction and reach of books.

choices.text: Handwritten books were always more beautiful than printed ones.
choices.rationale: Beauty of handwriting is not the passage's claim.

choices.text: Books became cheap immediately after the press was invented.
choices.rationale: The passage explicitly says costs did not fall overnight.

choices.text: Scribes disappeared the moment the printing press arrived.
choices.rationale: It notes scholars still prized handwritten volumes, so scribes did not vanish at once.

questions.explanation: The passage frames the press as a turning point in how widely texts could circulate.

stimulus.title: Letters of Lead
stimulus.text: Before the 1450s, a book in Europe was copied by hand, a task that could occupy a scribe for a year. Johannes Gutenberg changed that arithmetic. By casting individual letters in metal, he could arrange them into a page, print hundreds of copies, then rearrange the same letters for the next page. The cost of a book did not fall overnight, and many scholars still prized handwritten volumes. But within fifty years, presses across the continent had produced millions of books. Ideas that once crept from city to city now traveled in bulk, and a reader in one town could hold the exact words read by a stranger far away.

questions.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: The octopus rapidly changes color through a muscular pigment system it cannot itself see.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage centers on the surprising mechanism of color change in a colorblind animal.

choices.text: The octopus is the only sea creature that lacks bones.
choices.rationale: The passage never claims octopuses are unique in lacking bones.

choices.text: Researchers have fully explained how octopus skin detects light.
choices.rationale: The passage calls the skin-sensing idea a suspicion, not a settled explanation.

choices.text: Octopuses prefer to hide near gray stones rather than red coral.
choices.rationale: No preference for gray over red is described; both are examples of camouflage.

questions.explanation: The passage builds toward the paradox of an animal that produces colors it cannot perceive.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.

questions.prompt: The comparison to a fire extinguisher mainly suggests that the vault is

choices.text: valuable because it is ready even if rarely used
choices.rationale: Correct. Like an extinguisher, its worth lies in readiness for a rare emergency.
No danger is implied.
The passage hopes it is rarely needed.

choices.text: useful only for putting out fires
choices.rationale: The analogy is about readiness, not literal firefighting.

questions.explanation: The analogy stresses preparedness for an uncommon emergency.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that, before automation, a lighthouse's reliability depended on

choices.text: the constant labor of a human keeper
choices.rationale: Correct. Keepers performed the nightly tasks that kept the light burning.
Storms occurred and made the work dangerous.
Timers and sensors came later, with automation.

choices.text: the height of the tower alone
choices.rationale: Height alone did not keep the light lit.

questions.explanation: Reliability rested on the keeper's constant labor.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that mixing glass colors during recycling

choices.text: reduces the usefulness of the recycled material
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says mixing colors lowers the value of recycled glass.

choices.text: improves the strength of new bottles
choices.rationale: Mixing lowers value; it does not improve strength.
Sorting is recommended, not color mixing.
Mixing clearly has a negative effect.

questions.explanation: Mixed colors lower the recycled material's value, the passage states.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the angle of the waggle run is meaningful because it

choices.text: relates the direction of food to the position of the sun
choices.rationale: Correct. The angle against gravity encodes direction relative to the sun.

choices.text: shows the color of the flowers
choices.rationale: Color is not encoded by the dance.

choices.text: indicates the temperature of the hive
choices.rationale: Temperature is not mentioned.

choices.text: measures the age of the dancing bee
choices.rationale: The bee's age is irrelevant to the dance.

questions.explanation: The angle's meaning comes from its relation to the sun's direction.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the 'clean line' on a finished map represents

choices.text: a question that was eventually answered through exploration
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says every clean line 'was once a question,' settled by explorers.

choices.text: a deliberate decoration like a sea monster
choices.rationale: Clean lines replaced decorations, not added them.

choices.text: a region that remains entirely unknown
choices.rationale: Clean lines mark known, corrected regions.

choices.text: a mistake no one ever corrected
choices.rationale: The line marks a settled answer, not an uncorrected mistake.

questions.explanation: A clean line stands for a once-open question now resolved.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that fog harvesting is most practical in places that have

choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says it works only where fog is frequent and winds steady.
Such places would not need fog harvesting as much.
Steady winds are required, so 'no wind' is wrong.
Cold winters are not mentioned as a requirement.

questions.explanation: Suitability depends on frequent fog and steady wind.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

questions.prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the library obtained many of its scrolls by

choices.text: copying texts that arrived by ship
choices.rationale: Correct. Scrolls aboard docking ships were searched and copied.

choices.text: purchasing them only from local sellers
choices.rationale: The passage describes copying ships' scrolls, not only local purchases.
Scholars were welcomed, not forbidden.
Copying, not memory, is described.

questions.explanation: The ship-copying practice explains how the collection grew.

stimulus.title: A House for Every Book
stimulus.text: The ancient library at Alexandria aimed at something audacious: to gather a copy of every book in the world. Ships docking at the harbor were searched, and any scrolls aboard were copied; sometimes the library kept the original and returned the copy. Scholars from distant lands came to read, argue, and write, and the collection may have held hundreds of thousands of scrolls. We know less than we would like about how the library declined, and popular stories of a single great fire oversimplify a slow loss across centuries. Still, the idea behind it endured. The dream of one place that holds all knowledge has reappeared, in new forms, ever since.

questions.prompt: The passage most strongly implies that, regarding health effects, scientists are

choices.text: cautious because the evidence is still incomplete
choices.rationale: Correct. They 'caution' that effects are 'not yet clear.'

choices.text: certain that there is no risk
choices.rationale: They do not claim there is no risk.

choices.text: certain that the risk is severe
choices.rationale: They warn against easy conclusions, severe or not.
Their cautions show interest, not indifference.

questions.explanation: The hedged language signals careful uncertainty.

What is clear is that the cheapest fix is also the simplest: keeping plastic out of the environment in the first place, before it can shatter into pieces no net can catch.

choices.text: earlier technical improvements like pedals, chains, and tires
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage links each improvement to new riders before the craze.
Roads did not disappear; rough roads were a problem.
The bicycle's appeal was needing no horse.
The design changed substantially from 1817.

questions.explanation: Popularity followed the accumulation of improvements.

choices.text: are unaware that volcanoes can erupt

choices.text: harvest crops only in years without eruptions
choices.rationale: No such yearly pattern is stated.

choices.text: prefer poor soil to rich soil
choices.rationale: They value the rich soil, not poor soil.

choices.text: identical for every person regardless of timing
choices.rationale: It mentions 'the right times,' implying timing matters.

Drying into a husk is not growth.
It can be revived, so it is not dead.
No social behavior is described.

choices.text: caused by the surrounding water temperature

choices.text: learned slowly over the animal's lifetime
choices.rationale: The change happens in under a second, not slowly over a lifetime.
The passage does not say only octopuses can see the colors.

questions.explanation: The text directly ties color change to muscles pulling on pigment sacs.

choices.text: Even after decades, the seeds can still sprout.
choices.rationale: Correct. Sprouting after decades shows preservation of viability.

choices.text: The vault is built inside a frozen mountain.
choices.rationale: The location alone does not show preserved viability.

choices.text: The seeds come from around the world.
choices.rationale: The seeds' origins do not address viability.
Calling it a backup states purpose, not preservation.

questions.explanation: Preserved viability is shown by sprouting after decades.

choices.text: The lights still shine, more reliably than ever.
choices.rationale: Correct. 'More reliably than ever' directly supports improved reliability.
Dangerous storms concern the old hardship, not reliability gains.
Withdrawal of keepers is the human cost, not the reliability claim.
Loneliness describes the old role, not reliability.

questions.explanation: Improved reliability is stated directly in that detail.

stimulus.title: Keeping the Light
stimulus.text: For centuries, a lighthouse meant a keeper. Someone had to climb the tower each night, trim the wick, polish the lens, and wind the clockwork that turned the light. Storms made the work dangerous, and isolation made it lonely, but ships depended on that faithful beam. In the twentieth century, automation arrived. Electric lamps, timers, and remote sensors took over the tasks one by one, and keepers were gradually withdrawn. The lights still shine, more reliably than ever, yet something human slipped away with the last keeper. The machines kept the promise of the light; they could not keep its company.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that current systems limit glass recycling?

choices.text: Much glass ends up in landfills because collection is uneven.
choices.rationale: Correct. Uneven collection sending glass to landfills shows a system limitation.

choices.text: A bottle can become a bottle next month.
choices.rationale: This describes the ideal, not the limit.

choices.text: Glass can be melted down without losing quality.
choices.rationale: Quality retention is a property of glass, not a system failure.
'The material is willing' is figurative, not specific evidence.

questions.explanation: System limits are supported by the uneven-collection detail.

stimulus.title: The Endless Material
stimulus.text: Glass has a quiet superpower: it can be melted down and remade again and again without losing quality. A bottle recycled today can become a bottle next month, then another after that, in a loop that, in principle, never wears out. Compared with this ideal, reality lags. Much glass still ends up in landfills because collection is uneven and because mixing colors lowers the value of the recycled material. Sorting by color and keeping glass out of general trash would let more of it re-enter the loop. The material is willing; it is our systems, the author suggests, that need redesigning.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that the dance conveys distance?

choices.text: The length of the run signals distance.
choices.rationale: Correct. Run length signaling distance directly supports the distance claim.

choices.text: The bee dances on a vertical comb in darkness.
choices.rationale: The setting does not address distance.

choices.text: Bees crowd around and follow the dancer.
choices.rationale: Followers show interest, not the distance code.
Absence of sound concerns the medium, not distance.

questions.explanation: Distance is supported by the 'length of the run' detail.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that maps improved as exploration advanced?

choices.text: As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected.
choices.rationale: Correct. Corrections following explorers' measurements show improvement over time.

choices.text: Some maps added sea monsters as decoration.
choices.rationale: Decorative monsters do not show improvement.

choices.text: A finished map can hide how hard it was to make.
choices.rationale: Hidden difficulty is a reflection, not evidence of improvement.

choices.text: Mapmakers filled blank spaces with imagined mountains.
choices.rationale: Imagined mountains describe the early errors, not the improvement.

questions.explanation: Improvement is supported by corrections from returning explorers.

stimulus.title: The Edge of the Map
stimulus.text: Old maps are full of guesses. Where knowledge ran out, mapmakers filled the blank spaces with mountains they had never seen and coastlines that bent the wrong way. Some added sea monsters, less from belief than from a need to decorate the unknown. As explorers returned with measurements, the guesses were slowly corrected, and the monsters swam off the page. But a finished, accurate map can hide how hard it was to make. Every clean line was once a question, settled only by someone willing to sail past the edge of what was known and come back with an answer.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that fog harvesting can meaningfully add to a water supply?

choices.text: A single large net can collect many liters on a good day.
choices.rationale: Correct. The volume collected shows a meaningful contribution.
Upkeep is a limitation, not a benefit.

choices.text: It works only where fog is frequent.
choices.rationale: Frequency is a condition, not the yield.

choices.text: Moist air rolls in from the sea.
choices.rationale: Incoming moist air explains the source, not the amount collected.

questions.explanation: The 'many liters' figure supports the usefulness claim.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that the library's decline is poorly understood?

choices.text: We know less than we would like about how the library declined.
choices.rationale: Correct. This statement directly expresses limited knowledge of the decline.

choices.text: Ships' scrolls were searched and copied.
choices.rationale: Copying scrolls concerns growth, not decline.
Visiting scholars do not address the decline.

choices.text: The collection may have held hundreds of thousands of scrolls.
choices.rationale: Collection size does not address the decline.

questions.explanation: The uncertainty claim is stated directly in that sentence.

stimulus.title: A House for Every Book
stimulus.text: The ancient library at Alexandria aimed at something audacious: to gather a copy of every book in the world. Ships docking at the harbor were searched, and any scrolls aboard were copied; sometimes the library kept the original and returned the copy. Scholars from distant lands came to read, argue, and write, and the collection may have held hundreds of thousands of scrolls. We know less than we would like about how the library declined, and popular stories of a single great fire oversimplify a slow loss across centuries. Still, the idea behind it endured. The dream of one place that holds all knowledge has reappeared, in new forms, ever since.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that microplastics are widespread?

choices.text: They have turned up in river water, Arctic ice, and the bodies of fish and birds.
choices.rationale: Correct. The list of far-flung locations directly shows how widespread they are.
The 'fix' detail concerns response, not spread.

choices.text: Health effects are not yet clear.
choices.rationale: Uncertain health effects do not address spread.

choices.text: Plastic breaks down under sunlight and waves.
choices.rationale: Breakdown explains origin, not the geographic reach.

questions.explanation: Their spread is supported by the list of places they appear.

stimulus.title: Small Pieces, Wide Reach
stimulus.text: Plastic does not simply vanish. Exposed to sunlight and waves, a bottle or bag breaks into ever-smaller fragments until the pieces are too tiny to see. These microplastics have turned up in river water, in Arctic ice, and even in the bodies of fish and birds. Because the particles are so small, filtering them out of the ocean is nearly impossible. Researchers caution that the full effects on health are not yet clear, and they warn against easy conclusions. What is clear is that the cheapest fix is also the simplest: keeping plastic out of the environment in the first place, before it can shatter into pieces no net can catch.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that the bicycle offered unusual freedom of movement?

choices.text: It offered cheap, independent travel that needed no horse and no rails.
choices.rationale: Correct. Independent travel without horse or rails directly shows the freedom it offered.

choices.text: It was built in 1817 as a wooden frame.
choices.rationale: Its construction date does not address freedom.
Mockery concerns the early version's reception.

choices.text: Rutted roads made the first version impractical.
choices.rationale: Poor roads describe an obstacle, not the freedom.

questions.explanation: Freedom of movement is supported by the 'no horse and no rails' detail.

stimulus.title: The Machine That Walked
stimulus.text: The earliest ancestor of the bicycle had no pedals at all. Built in 1817, it was a wooden frame on two wheels that a rider straddled and pushed along with the feet, like walking while seated. People mocked it, and rutted roads made it impractical, so it soon faded. Decades passed before inventors added pedals, then a chain, then air-filled tires that smoothed the ride. Each change drew new riders, and by the 1890s the bicycle had become a craze. It offered something rare for the time: cheap, independent travel that needed no horse and no rails. For many people, especially those long denied easy movement, the machine quietly widened the boundaries of daily life.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that volcanic regions attract dense settlement?

choices.text: Slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages.
choices.rationale: Correct. 'Crowded with farms and villages' directly shows dense settlement.
Mineral content explains fertility, not settlement density.

choices.text: The same mountain can threaten the fields.
choices.rationale: The threat detail concerns risk, not attraction.

choices.text: Ash takes years to break down.
choices.rationale: Breakdown time does not address settlement.

questions.explanation: Settlement density is shown by the 'crowded' detail.

stimulus.title: Gifts of the Volcano
stimulus.text: It seems strange that people would farm in the shadow of a volcano, but many do, and for good reason. When a volcano erupts, it scatters ash rich in minerals such as potassium and phosphorus. Over years, this ash breaks down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. Crops planted in it can grow unusually well, which is why slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages. The bargain is uneasy: the same mountain that feeds the fields can also threaten them. Farmers weigh the steady gift of rich soil against the rare but real danger overhead, and generation after generation, many choose to stay.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that sleep aids memory?

choices.text: Students who slept after studying remembered more words than those who stayed awake.
choices.rationale: Correct. The comparison of sleepers to non-sleepers directly supports the memory claim.

choices.text: Waves of activity sweep across the cortex.
choices.rationale: The waves describe a mechanism, not the memory outcome.
Waste clearance is a separate benefit.
The curve concerns amount, not the memory result itself.

questions.explanation: The strongest support is the experimental comparison of recall.

stimulus.title: The Night Shift of the Brain
stimulus.text: Sleep can look like idleness, but the brain is busy. During deep sleep, waves of electrical activity sweep across the cortex, and researchers think these waves help move the day's memories into longer-term storage. In one line of experiments, students who slept after studying a list of words remembered more of them than students who stayed awake the same number of hours. Sleep may also clear away molecular waste that builds up while we are awake. None of this means more sleep is always better; the relationship appears to follow a curve, with both too little and too much linked to poorer performance. What the brain seems to need is enough, at the right times.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that song is learned rather than innate?

choices.text: A bird that hears no song in its early window sings only a thin, simplified tune.
choices.rationale: Correct. Deprivation of song leading to a poor tune shows learning is required.

choices.text: The basic melody is the same across regions.
choices.rationale: Shared melody alone does not prove learning.

choices.text: A recording may sound foreign decades later.
choices.rationale: Drift over decades concerns change, not whether song is learned.
Habitat does not address learning.

questions.explanation: The deprivation result is the key evidence that song is learned.

stimulus.title: Songs With Accents
stimulus.text: A white-crowned sparrow raised near San Francisco does not sing quite like one raised a hundred miles north. The basic melody is the same, but small flourishes differ from place to place, forming what biologists call song dialects. Young sparrows learn these dialects by listening to adult males during a brief window in their first months. A bird that hears no song in that window grows up to sing a thin, simplified tune. The dialects can drift over generations, much as human accents do, so a recording made decades ago may sound subtly foreign to the birds singing in the same meadow today.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that Nadia's playing improved on the loaner?

choices.text: Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily.
choices.rationale: Correct. The ease of once-difficult notes shows improved playing.

choices.text: Two of the pegs slipped during every lesson.
choices.rationale: Slipping pegs describe the old instrument, not improvement.

choices.text: She returned the violin to its case.
choices.rationale: Returning the violin does not show improved playing.
Her near-refusal is about hesitation, not improvement.

questions.explanation: Improvement is shown by once-hard notes arriving easily.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that the tardigrade's revival can occur after a long delay?

choices.text: Years later, a single drop of water can revive it.
choices.rationale: Correct. 'Years later' directly establishes the long delay before revival.

choices.text: It plods on eight stubby legs.
choices.rationale: Its legs are unrelated to revival timing.

choices.text: It is smaller than a grain of salt.
choices.rationale: Its size does not address the delay.

questions.explanation: The long-delay claim rests on the 'years later' detail.

stimulus.title: The Survivor
stimulus.text: Smaller than a grain of salt, the tardigrade looks almost comical, plodding on eight stubby legs. Its talent, however, is anything but ordinary. When its surroundings dry out, the creature expels most of its water and curls into a husk called a tun. In this state it can endure boiling heat, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill nearly any other animal. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it. Scientists are still untangling how the tardigrade protects its cells, but one clue is a special set of proteins that seem to harden into glass, cradling delicate structures until water returns.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that printing dramatically increased the number of books?

choices.text: Within fifty years, presses had produced millions of books.
choices.rationale: Correct. The figure of millions in fifty years directly shows the surge in output.

choices.text: A scribe could spend a year on one copy.
choices.rationale: This describes the old method, not the increase.

choices.text: Many scholars still prized handwritten volumes.
choices.rationale: This shows continued demand for manuscripts, not the scale of printing.

choices.text: Gutenberg cast individual letters in metal.
choices.rationale: This explains the method but not the resulting quantity.

questions.explanation: The claim about volume is supported by the 'millions of books' figure.

stimulus.title: Letters of Lead
stimulus.text: Before the 1450s, a book in Europe was copied by hand, a task that could occupy a scribe for a year. Johannes Gutenberg changed that arithmetic. By casting individual letters in metal, he could arrange them into a page, print hundreds of copies, then rearrange the same letters for the next page. The cost of a book did not fall overnight, and many scholars still prized handwritten volumes. But within fifty years, presses across the continent had produced millions of books. Ideas that once crept from city to city now traveled in bulk, and a reader in one town could hold the exact words read by a stranger far away.

questions.prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that the octopus cannot perceive its own display?

choices.text: The statement that the octopus is colorblind.
choices.rationale: Correct. Colorblindness directly explains why it cannot see the hues it makes.

choices.text: The statement that it has no bones.
choices.rationale: Lacking bones is unrelated to perceiving color.

choices.text: The statement that color blooms in under a second.
choices.rationale: Speed of change does not address perception.

choices.text: The statement that pigment sacs are ringed by muscle.
choices.rationale: The muscle ring explains how color appears, not whether the animal sees it.

questions.explanation: The claim about perception is supported specifically by the detail that the animal is colorblind.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.

stimulus.title: A Vault for Seeds
stimulus.text: Deep inside a frozen mountain, a vault holds something more valuable than gold: seeds. Hundreds of thousands of varieties of crops, gathered from around the world, rest in sealed packets at temperatures far below freezing. The cold slows the seeds' aging to a crawl, so that even after decades they can still sprout. The vault is a backup. If a war, flood, or disease wipes out a crop somewhere, a sample can be withdrawn and grown again. Its keepers hope it will rarely be needed, the way one hopes never to use a fire extinguisher. Its value lies precisely in being ready and almost never used.
